hook: The world's longest-running laboratory experiment: a blob of pitch dripping through a funnel since 1930, on public view at UQ.
facts: Started in 1927 by Thomas Parnell, the University of Queensland's first Professor of Physics, to show that pitch, though it appears solid and shatters under a hammer, is actually a very high-viscosity fluid.
The funnel's stem was cut in 1930, and the pitch has dripped ever since; pitch is estimated to be around 100 billion times more viscous than water.
It took about eight years for the first drop to fall, and only nine drops have fallen in total, with the tenth still forming.
Recognised by the Guinness Book of Records as the longest continuously running laboratory experiment in the world.

It is displayed in a cabinet in the foyer of the Parnell Building (Building 7), School of Mathematics and Physics, St Lucia campus, opposite the Physics Museum, and a live video feed runs on the university website.
w: In a quiet university foyer sits the world's longest-running lab experiment: a funnel of tar-like pitch, set going in 1927, that drips about once a decade. In nearly a century only nine drops have fallen — and, famously, no one has ever caught one in the act. A webcam now watches around the clock. Science at the speed of geology.

hook: A neon-lit parabolic arch and ceramic dancefloor memorialising the beloved Cloudland Ballroom, controversially demolished in a 1982 midnight raid.
facts: The memorial commemorates the Cloudland Ballroom, a landmark Brisbane dance hall whose glowing parabolic arch was visible across the city for over 40 years.
Cloudland was demolished by the Deen Brothers in the early hours of 7 November 1982 during the Bjelke-Petersen era, an act that became a symbol of Brisbane heritage loss.
The memorial arch was designed by public artist Jamie MacLean and erected in 2004, echoing the original parabolic entry.
It presides over a ceramic dancefloor imprinted with dancing footsteps and recollections from people who attended the ballroom.
It stands in a small wedge of parkland on Cowlishaw Street, Bowen Hills, below the hilltop site of the original ballroom, and was restored in December 2024.
w: One arch is all that survives of Cloudland, the glamorous hilltop ballroom where Brisbane danced for half a century. The Deen Brothers tore the rest down overnight in 1982, a notorious midnight demolition symbolising the Bjelke-Petersen years. The lonely arch now marks a unit block. A city's lost nights, in one curve of concrete.

hook: Queensland's largest cemetery, 44 hectares of Victorian monuments, home to the city's most famous 'gravity hill' urban legend.
facts: Established in 1866 and formally opened in 1875, the Brisbane General Cemetery at Toowong is Queensland's largest cemetery at about 44 hectares, with over 120,000 people interred.
Notable burials include Queensland governor Samuel Wensley Blackall, Australian Prime Minister Frank Forde, pioneering doctor Lilian Cooper, and boxer Peter Jackson.
The cemetery contains roughly 270 World War I and 117 World War II war graves and was the focal point for Brisbane's Anzac Day ceremonies from 1920 to 1930.
It is located on the corner of Frederick Street and Mt Coot-tha Road, Toowong, about 4.5 km west of central Brisbane, and is run by Brisbane City Council.
'Spook Hill' is the local name for the hill that Twelfth Avenue crosses within the grounds.
legend: Urban legend holds that a car left in neutral at the foot of Spook Hill on Twelfth Avenue will roll uphill; this is a gravity-hill optical illusion and testing has not reproduced the effect as described.
One tale attributes the 'uphill pull' to the ghosts of two sisters said to have died in a car crash, dragging cars up to share their fate.

A separate vampire legend claims a woman's exhumed body was found preserved with fang-like teeth, after which a 19th-century-dressed apparition was reported near Spook Hill; the cemetery is a long-running ghost-tour destination.
w: Brisbane's grand Victorian necropolis climbs a hillside thick with weathered angels and the city's founding names. On one avenue, cars left out of gear appear to roll uphill — 'Spook Hill,' blamed on ghosts but really a gravity-hill illusion. Ghost tours work the older sections after dark. Beautiful, overgrown and quietly eerie.

hook: Queensland's oldest occupied building, a convict-built 1829 store on William Street now packed with penal-colony relics.
facts: Built between 1828 and 1829 by convict labour under Captain Patrick Logan, to a design by William John Dumaresq, as the Commissariat Store for the Moreton Bay penal settlement.
It is Queensland's oldest continually occupied building and one of only two surviving convict-era buildings in the state (the other being the nearby Old Windmill).
A third storey was added in 1913.
Since 1976 it has been the headquarters of the Royal Historical Society of Queensland and operates as a museum, with the Welsby Library and function spaces.
Its exhibits cover convict and colonial Queensland history, displayed across the ground, first and part of the second floors.
It stands at 115 William Street in the Brisbane CBD, near the Queen's Wharf precinct.
w: Convicts cut the stone for this riverside store in 1829, making it the oldest occupied building in Queensland. Three floors now hold the colony's relics — leg irons, a treadmill model, the grim machinery of a penal outpost. Run by the Royal Historical Society. The city's hard beginnings, under one roof.

hook: A glass cabinet of contraband seized from smugglers at the border, including a taxidermied mongoose locked in mid-fight with a cobra.
facts: The display consists of endangered-species products and live-animal contraband confiscated from travellers by border officials at Brisbane International Terminal.
Its contents include unusual seized items such as a taxidermied mongoose mounted mid-fight with a cobra.
It serves as a public deterrent reminding travellers that trading endangered species can bring heavy fines or imprisonment under Australia's CITES obligations.
It is located in the international terminal near the departure-gate level; visitors in the arrivals area can reportedly take the lift up to the departures level to view it without a boarding pass.
w: Between the terminals at Brisbane Airport stands a glass case of crime: ivory, snakeskin, a mongoose locked forever in combat with a cobra, all seized from travellers smuggling protected wildlife. Customs built the display as a warning. You can view it landside, no boarding pass needed. Souvenirs that cost an animal its life.

hook: Concrete-and-brick blast stands where WWII fighter engines were run flat-out, now the last relics of Brisbane's wartime airfield.
facts: Built circa 1942-43 in three stages by the USAAF 81st Air Depot Group and the Allied Works Council for static testing of Allison aircraft engines overhauled at the nearby GM-Holden's plant at Breakfast Creek.
They were part of Eagle Farm Aerodrome, a major base when Australia served as a stronghold for US operations in the Pacific during World War II.
Surviving fabric includes two enclosed brick structures and six open concrete stands, the only Brisbane test site incorporating three different stand designs.
Along with Hangar 7, the stands are among the only structures remaining from the site's WWII airfield use.
They were added to the Queensland Heritage Register on 5 August 2003 and now sit within TradeCoast Central Heritage Park, with interpretive displays, adjacent to 71 Amy Johnson Place.
w: Three hulking concrete bays at Eagle Farm, built in 1943 to test and run-in the Allison engines of American warplanes before they flew off to the Pacific. The roar must have been tremendous; now they stand silent and heritage-listed. Few relics speak so plainly of Brisbane's wartime role. Aviation history in raw concrete.

hook: General Douglas MacArthur's preserved wartime office on the 8th floor of his old Allied HQ, the nerve centre of the South West Pacific.
facts: Housed in MacArthur Chambers at 201 Edward Street (corner of Queen Street), a building constructed 1931-1934 as the Queensland headquarters of the AMP Society.
General Douglas MacArthur used the building as Allied South West Pacific Area headquarters from July 1942 to roughly 1944, with tenants compulsorily evacuated under military orders.
The museum's centrepiece is MacArthur's preserved office on the 8th floor.
It focuses on Brisbane's role in WWII from 1939-1945 and the wartime Australia-US alliance; up to a million US personnel passed through the city en route to the Pacific.
The MacArthur Museum opened on 15 August 2004.
w: From the eighth floor of this CBD building, General Douglas MacArthur ran the Allied war in the South-West Pacific from 1942. His preserved office and a compact museum now tell the story of Brisbane as wartime command centre. Easy to walk past the heritage doorway. The war room above the shopfronts.

n: Underground Opera at the Spring Hill Reservoir
hook: Opera performed six metres underground inside an 1870s decommissioned water reservoir in Wickham Park.
facts: Held in the Spring Hill Reservoirs (officially 'Service Reservoirs'), two heritage-listed former underground water-storage reservoirs at 230 Wickham Terrace within Wickham Park, near the Old Windmill.
Listed on the Queensland Heritage Register on 21 October 1992 (ref 600174); the fabric dates to 1871 and 1882, with the reservoirs supplying Brisbane's water until the 1960s.
The Underground Opera Company first staged performances in the reservoir in 2014, after spending around three years cleaning the space.
Audiences descend roughly six metres below Wickham Terrace via a metal staircase into the intimate vaulted chamber, with a small wine bar before seating.
The Underground Opera Company is a Brisbane-based professional company that produces performances in unusual venues across Australia, with seasons running on selected dates each year.
w: Beneath a Spring Hill park lie the brick chambers of a Victorian water reservoir, cool, pillared and acoustically uncanny. Decommissioned long ago, it now fills a few weeks a year with the Underground Opera season, voices ringing off the waterworks. Audiences descend by torchlight to sit among the columns. Arias, two storeys underground.

hook: A 1920s whisky-and-jazz speakeasy reached through a padlocked wardrobe, hidden above Electric Avenue.
facts: Located on the top floor of Electric Avenue at 23 Logan Road, Woolloongabba, entered through an old wardrobe/armoire that requires a password obtained from staff downstairs.
Styled as a 1920s speakeasy with moody lighting, dark-wood bar, leather armchairs, art deco chandeliers and curiosities lining the walls.
Cocktails are named for fairytale characters and served in teacups and vintage glassware; the bar specialises in gin and luxe whisky.
Reported open from 6pm Thursday to Sunday; review/listing data updated into March 2026 confirms it is currently operating.
Food offerings include warmed olives with garlic-butter bread, shoestring fries, chicken pate, and cheese/charcuterie boards.
w: Step through an antique wardrobe in a Woolloongabba pub and tumble, Narnia-style, into a hidden speakeasy of dim lamps, deep armchairs and rare whiskies. Mrs J. Rabbits plays its storybook conceit straight, down to the fur coats you brush past on the way in. Find the wardrobe, then find a seat. A drink at the back of the closet.

hook: Retro arcade bar with 25 arcade machines, 15 pinball tables, 24 craft taps and an all-vegan diner.
facts: Located at 186 Brunswick Street, Fortitude Valley; billed as Brisbane's first arcade-game bar.
Holds around 25 arcade machines, 15 pinball machines, classic consoles and board games, including Space Invaders, Frogger, Donkey Kong and Twilight Zone/Addams Family pinball.
Pours 24 taps of local beer, cider and mixed drinks; the in-house Hellmouth Diner serves vegan, mostly gluten-free American-Japanese food with optional dairy/meat add-ons.
Open Tuesday to Sunday; Yelp listing updated June 2026 confirms it is currently operating.
The same team runs Lost Souls Karaoke, a connected purgatory-themed karaoke bar with private rooms.
w: A neon-lit temple to misspent youth: rows of retro arcade cabinets and pinball, shelves of board games, craft beer on tap and an all-vegan kitchen. Netherworld has anchored Fortitude Valley's geek scene for years, part bar, part games den. Sink a beer, lose a life on Street Fighter. Brisbane's friendliest portal to the underworld.

hook: Parisian steakhouse inside the notorious Fitzgerald-Inquiry brothel, casino and bathhouse building.
facts: Located on the corner of Wickham and Little Streets at 144 Wickham Street, Fortitude Valley.
Occupies the building that housed the illegal casino and the Bubbles Bath-House massage parlour/brothel central to the 1980s Fitzgerald Inquiry into Queensland police corruption.
Serves a fixed Parisian steakhouse format: green salad, house bread, grass-fed steak and bottomless frites with retro sauces.
A downstairs tiled 'Bubbles Bathhouse' bar retains a spa and mirrorball under the motto 'Anything Goes'; an exposed-brick wall carries a neon sign joking that it is no longer a brothel.
Yelp/Wheree listings updated to May 2026 confirm it is currently operating.
Decor includes portraits of Fitzgerald-era figures such as Joh Bjelke-Petersen, Russ Hinze and Terry Lewis.
legend: Period accounts hold that the bathhouse operated as a brothel beneath an illegal upstairs casino, said to have been run by Geraldo Bellino and Victor Conte during the era exposed by the Fitzgerald Inquiry.
w: A cheap-and-cheerful steakhouse with a deeply disreputable past: the building ran as an illegal casino, a bathhouse and a brothel through the corrupt years the Fitzgerald Inquiry later blew open. Old photos of its shady characters line the walls beside the steaks. Les Bubbles wears its history with a wink. Sin, served medium-rare.

hook: A back-street Albion warehouse that roasts coffee, brews beer and distils gin and vodka under one roof.
facts: Located in a warehouse at 40 Fox Street, Albion, overlooking Albion Park, combining roasting, brewing and distilling in a single venue.
The brand was founded by Dan around 2010, starting as a coffee roaster before expanding into an on-site brewery and then a distillery.
Distils gin and vodka on site using 100% Australian ingredients, sold by the can or bottle alongside premise-brewed beer.
Placed 70th in the inaugural World's 100 Best Coffee Shops awards.
Operates as a cafe/cellar-door venue serving coffee, beer, spirits and food, not solely a wholesale coffee brand.
w: One Albion warehouse out to fuel your whole day and night: it roasts its own coffee, brews its own beer and distils its own spirits, all under a single corrugated roof. Fonzie Abbott began as a roaster and kept adding trades until breakfast and last call shared an address. Caffeine by morning, gin by midnight. A one-stop vice shop.

hook: A 50-dealer antiques collective inside a 1929 heritage atmospheric cinema with a painted night-sky ceiling.
facts: Housed in the heritage-listed former Plaza Theatre at 163-169 Latrobe Terrace, Paddington, designed by Richard Gailey Jr and built in 1929.
The Plaza Theatre is on the Queensland Heritage Register (listed 28 March 2003, ref 601654) and is a rare suburban 'atmospheric' cinema with a painted blue night-sky ceiling.
An antiques centre (Paddington Antique Centre) opened in the building in 1985; Suzy Baines bought the business in 2008 and rebranded it Empire Revival in 2018.
Operates as a collective trading enterprise of around 50 merchants selling antiques, jewellery, fashion, furniture and homewares.
Promoted as one of Australia's longest-running antique collectives; Yelp listing updated June 2026 confirms it is currently operating.
w: Vintage dealers have colonised one of Brisbane's last grand picture palaces: a 1929 atmospheric theatre in Paddington, its starry ceiling now presiding over a maze of antiques. Empire Revival lets you browse old wares beneath the bones of a cinema. Half junk shop, half time machine. Lose an afternoon under the proscenium.

hook: A riverfront street-food market built from about 180 repurposed shipping containers, open Friday to Sunday.
facts: Located on the Northshore Hamilton riverfront at 221D Macarthur Avenue, Hamilton.
Built from roughly 180 repurposed shipping containers housing micro-restaurants, themed bars and shops across five entertainment stages.
Open Friday and Saturday 4pm-10pm and Sunday 4pm-9pm (seasonal variations apply).
Charges a $6 per-person entry fee, with free entry for carers and children under 13.
Hosts over 70 traders offering street food from multiple continents plus live entertainment.
w: A whole night market built from old shipping containers, strung with lights on the riverbank at Northshore Hamilton: dozens of stalls dishing up the food of the world, music spilling between them. Eat Street opens Friday to Sunday and charges a small fee at the gate. Graze your way down the rows. A disused wharf, reborn as a feast.

hook: Venetian-style covered gondola dining cruises that glide along the Brisbane River from a Newstead dock.
facts: Departs from a fixed dock at 40 Ross Street, Newstead, near the historic Breakfast Creek Wharf.
Runs Venetian-inspired gondolas fitted with an enclosed weatherproof cabin, sheepskin-covered seats and a soundproof panel separating guests from the gondolier for privacy.
Offers candlelit dining cruises with seafood menus and music, including evening dinner, anniversary, breakfast and midday options.
Confirmed currently operating: business listings were updated into late 2025 (Yelp May 2025, directory listings Dec 2025) with active booking and contact details.
Bookings are taken by phone (Gavin, 0419 400 944) and online, with reservations recommended.
w: Improbably, you can drift down a Brisbane creek in a real Venetian gondola, poled by a gondolier who may well serenade you. The Golden Gondola sets out from a Newstead dock near Breakfast Creek, threading the quiet water past mangroves and moored boats. Cheesy, romantic, entirely unexpected. Venice, by way of the subtropics.

hook: An 1880s pentagonal coastal fort hidden behind a moat and grassy ramparts, built to guard the mouth of the Brisbane River from naval attack.
facts: Fort Lytton was constructed in 1880-1882 at the southern bank of the Brisbane River mouth, about 13 km northeast of the Brisbane CBD, to deny enemy vessels access to the river.
It was built in response to fears that a foreign colonial power such as Russia or France might launch a naval attack on Brisbane and its port, and was designed by Lieutenant-Colonel Peter Scratchley.
It is a pentagonal fortress concealed behind a water-filled moat and grassy embankments, with heavy armaments hidden behind ramparts connected by underground passages.

From 1881 until the 1930s it was Brisbane's front line of defence and is regarded as the birthplace of Queensland's military history; after federation in 1901 it passed to the Commonwealth and stayed a military base until shortly after WWII.

In 1963 the Ampol oil company bought the surrounding land for the Lytton Oil Refinery; in 1988 Ampol transferred the parcel containing the original fort to the Queensland Government, and it became Fort Lytton National Park in 1990.
The park now offers guided and self-guided tours of the fort and military structures, a military museum and regular historic re-enactments.
w: Hidden behind grassy ramparts at the mouth of the Brisbane River, this pentagonal fort was dug in the 1880s to fend off a feared Russian invasion that never came. Its tunnels and disappearing cannon birthed Queensland's military. Volunteers still fire the guns on open days. A fortress camouflaged in a riverside field.

hook: A ruin-covered Moreton Bay island that was Queensland's first penal settlement, once nicknamed 'the hell hole of the Pacific' and now reachable only by tour boat.
facts: St Helena Island is a heritage-listed island in Moreton Bay, about 21 km east of Brisbane and roughly 4-8 km east of the mouth of the Brisbane River.

On 14 May 1867 the Governor of Queensland proclaimed the island a place to detain offenders under sentence of hard labour or penal servitude; it operated as a prison from 1867 until the last prisoner left on 15 February 1933.

By the turn of the 20th century it held over 300 prisoners inside a maze of buildings surrounded by a high stockade wall, and became largely self-sufficient, exporting bricks, clothing and sisal-hemp rope to Brisbane.
Surviving ruins include the prison stockade with bakery, workshops and underground tanks, plus stone ruins of warder accommodation, a sugar mill, lime kiln, jetty and Queensland's first tramway.
The island became a national park in 1979 and is accessible only by commercial tour boat, with guided tours of the ruins.
Despite its isolation there were fewer than 25 serious escape attempts during its 66 years of operation.
legend: The settlement earned the grim nickname 'the hell hole of the Pacific' for its harsh early conditions and punishment methods.
w: An island in Moreton Bay that the colony turned into its harshest prison — 'the hell-hole of the Pacific' — from 1867, where convicts farmed sugar and broke stone. The roofless ruins and old tramway now stand as a national park, reachable only by tour boat. Ghost tours run after dark. A penal colony, marooned on a green islet.

hook: At 351.5 m, Brisbane's longest historic timber pier reaches deep into Bramble Bay - a white-railed colonial promenade rebuilt in 2016 after marine borers ate the original.
facts: Shorncliffe Pier reaches 351.5 metres into Bramble Bay, making it the largest timber pier in Brisbane and one of the longest recreational piers in Australia.

Originally known as Sandgate Pier, it was built in 1872-1884 during the heyday of Sandgate's seaside tourism; designed by Colonial Architect F.D.G. Stanley, the structure was extended by about 91 metres in 1884 to give steam passenger ferries enough depth.

The Sandgate Pier Company Limited was formed on 16 February 1882 to build the pier, bathing houses, hotel and pavilions, backed by prominent Brisbane citizens including Thomas McIlwraith and Patrick Perkins.
In March 2012 the pier was closed for public safety after marine borers were found to have damaged the timber.
A rebuilt pier - with concrete and steel substructure but timber decking, handrails and a rotunda, on the original alignment - reopened to the public on Good Friday, 25 March 2016.
The pier forms part of the Bramble Bay Foreshores heritage listing on the Brisbane City Council heritage register.
w: Reaching some 350 metres into Moreton Bay, the longest timber pier in the region survives from Shorncliffe's heyday as a genteel Victorian seaside resort. Rebuilt plank by plank in 2016, it still draws anglers, swimmers and dawn walkers out over the water. Catch the sunrise from the far end. A wooden promenade into the bay.

hook: A small Moreton Bay island ringed by beaches with a tiny village, reachable by a short passenger ferry or vehicle barge from Victoria Point.
facts: Coochiemudlo Island is a small island in southern Moreton Bay, reached by a roughly 2.0 km ferry ride from the jetty at Victoria Point, about 12 km south-east of Cleveland.
The island's jetty and pontoon are in Elizabeth Street on the south side at 27 deg 34'29"S 153 deg 19'56"E, with a boat ramp at Tageruba Street nearby.
It is served by a passenger ferry running roughly every half-hour from about 5:00am to 11:00pm, plus a regular vehicular barge.

Doug Morton ran an unofficial ferry from 1948 and carried the island's mail from 1946; the first official weekend service began in 1959 when Dick Whitehall bought Morton's boat 'Koorooloo', with regular daily services from mid-1968.
A passenger ferry named 'Matthew Flinders' was launched in 1994 by Redland Mayor Eddie Santaguiliana, named for the explorer who landed on the island in 1799.
The island has beaches around its perimeter and a small residential village at its centre.
w: A pocket-sized island a short ferry from the mainland, ringed by quiet beaches and red ochre cliffs and almost untouched by development. Matthew Flinders landed here in 1799; today 'Coochie' keeps a slow village pace of bare feet, bikes and barbecues. Walk its whole shore in an afternoon. The Moreton Bay island the day-trippers forget.

hook: A quiet working-port riverside suburb on a narrow strip of land between the Brisbane River and the airport, anchored by the century-old Pinkenba Hotel ('The Pink').
facts: Pinkenba is a long narrow strip of land on the northern side of the Brisbane River facing Moreton Bay, about 10 km from the Brisbane CBD, dominated today by port and industrial uses.
The name derives from the Aboriginal word 'Binkinba', meaning 'place of tortoises', formally applied to the railway station when it opened in 1897.
The area was originally settled for farming, once extending over land now covered by Brisbane Airport and out toward Nudgee and the former Cribb Island.
The defensible riverside anchor is the Pinkenba Hotel at 144 Eagle Farm Road - established in 1910, long known as 'The Pink' and now operating as the Trade Coast Hotel.
Historic congregations included Church of England and Presbyterian churches; a Baptist church opened in Pinkenba in 1902, and a Presbyterian church opened in 1915 before being removed around 1980.
w: Out where the river meets the port, Pinkenba clings on as a sliver of old working Brisbane, hemmed in by wharves and tank farms. Its 1910 pub, the Pink, still serves the truckies and dock hands, and quiet lanes hold pockets of pre-industrial calm. An overlooked corner, hard and salty. The city's working edge, by the water.

hook: A glass hangar near Brisbane Airport housing the Southern Cross, the Fokker trimotor that made the first trans-Pacific flight to Australia in 1928.
facts: The memorial preserves the 'Southern Cross', a Fokker F.VIIb/3m trimotor monoplane, inside a purpose-built glass hangar on Airport Drive near the international terminal at Brisbane Airport.

The Southern Cross was flown by Charles Kingsford Smith, Charles Ulm, Harry Lyon and James Warner on the first trans-Pacific flight to Australia, from Oakland/San Francisco, California, landing at Eagle Farm at 10:50 am on 9 June 1928 after about 11,670 km.
An estimated 25,000 people were waiting at Eagle Farm to greet the aircraft's arrival.
The memorial building, designed by Noel Robinson Architects, was completed in 1988; its glass walls use overhangs and vertical fins to shield the aircraft from ultraviolet light and glare.
Brisbane Airport is the custodian of the aircraft on behalf of the Commonwealth of Australia, and the memorial is free and open 24/7.
w: Under a glass canopy near the airport rests the Southern Cross, the Fokker monoplane in which Charles Kingsford Smith made the first flight across the Pacific in 1928. Brisbane-born, he vanished over the Andaman Sea in 1935. The memorial keeps his battered, record-breaking machine on quiet display. The plane that shrank an ocean.

hook: A tucked-away remnant of Brisbane's lost Three Mile Scrub rainforest, threaded with bush tracks just 5km from the CBD.
facts: A bushland reserve in Alderley spanning over 20 hectares along Enoggera Creek, roughly 5km from Brisbane's city centre, managed by Brisbane City Council.
The land is a surviving fragment of the 'Three Mile Scrub' rainforest that covered the area in the 1800s, ranging from wet-rainforest creek flats to dry rainforest on the higher slopes.
Brisbane City Council began progressively acquiring the land that forms the reserve from 1939.
In the mid-20th century part of the site was worked as Chinese market gardens, which were destroyed in the 1974 Brisbane floods and never revived.
It carries bushwalking and bike tracks plus picnic areas, and supports wildlife including boobook owls, sacred kingfishers, pale-headed rosellas, eastern water dragons and eastern whipbirds.
Public access and on-street parking are off Weatherhead Avenue, Banks Street and View Street; leashed dogs are permitted.
w: A small bush reserve folded into the suburban streets of Alderley, all gum trees and birdsong, that locals guard as their 'little bit of magic.' Volunteers spent years coaxing the native scrub back; few outsiders know it exists. Slip in for a quiet wander among the eucalypts. A secret pocket of bushland, hidden in plain suburbia.

hook: Brisbane's oldest laneway, a former convict exercise yard now strung with tiny street-art doors, cafes and cocktail bars.
facts: A narrow CBD laneway running between the Queen Street Mall block and Adelaide Street, regarded as Brisbane's oldest lane and dating from the convict era.
It is named after James Burnett, Brisbane's first surveyor, who died at 39.

During the penal-colony years the block it cuts through was the site of the Prisoners' Cells and Barracks and the Superintendent of Convicts' residence; it later served as the tradesmen's entrance to Queen and Adelaide Street buildings.
Brisbane City Council announced a refurbishment in 2008, integrating the lane into the Queen Street Mall precinct and seeding its cafe-and-bar revival.
It hosts bars including Death & Taxes and Super Whatnot and eateries such as Felix for Goodness, and has become a street-art hub.
Guerilla artist Mace Robertson installed the first of his miniature painted 'tiny doors' here in 2017; the lane also features bird works by Blu Art Xinja.
legend: It is widely said the lane was used for floggings and hangings during the penal-colony period.
w: The city's oldest laneway, sketched onto the convict-era map of the 1820s and long a grubby service alley, now hums with hidden bars, cafes and street art. Burnett Lane shows how Brisbane learned to love its back lanes, stringing lights down a once-forgotten slot. A short cut with a long memory. The CBD's secret seam.

hook: A 450-metre open-air gallery of murals, sculpture and light installations woven through South Brisbane's bars and restaurants.
facts: A laneway running roughly 450 metres through South Brisbane towards West End, beside the cultural precinct of QAGOMA, the Queensland Museum and QPAC.
Originally called Soda Water Lane in the 1800s, it serviced the working docks of South Brisbane and was renamed in 1906 to honour Brisbane City Council Alderman and businessman George Fish.
Developer Aria established the Fish Lane Arts Precinct in 2019 as a dining, art and culture quarter.
Its public art includes a geometric light work by Kuuki, Nike Savvas' array of acrylic discs, Bruno Catalano's 'Raphael, The Traveller' sculpture and Avery's 'Steam Machine' aluminium installation.
Murals along the lane are by artists including Fintan Magee, Sofles, Arina Apostolova, Warraba Weatherall and Daniel Jones.
It is lined with bars and restaurants and hosts festivals and events as part of the precinct.
w: Once a back lane of loading docks under the rail viaduct, Fish Lane has bloomed into South Brisbane's most colourful corridor — murals, fairy-lit dining and pocket bars by the cultural precinct. Artists repaint its walls, so it is never quite the same twice. Wander through after dark. A working lane turned open-air canvas.

hook: A native-wildlife centre and platypus house on the shore of Enoggera Reservoir, the gateway into D'Aguilar National Park.
facts: A wildlife and visitor centre on the southern edge of D'Aguilar National Park at 60 Mount Nebo Road, Enoggera Reservoir, about 12km from Brisbane's city centre.
It is run by Queensland's parks service and houses reptiles, amphibians, fish and mammals, including a platypus and an outdoor walk-through kangaroo and wallaby habitat.
Wildlife on display includes snakes, turtles, freshwater crocodiles, lizards and wombats, with Wildlife Discovery Shows held every weekend at 11am.
It serves as the gateway to the 36,400-hectare D'Aguilar National Park of eucalypt forest and rainforest, stretching from The Gap to Lake Wivenhoe and Mount Mee.
Adjacent Enoggera Reservoir offers swimming, kayaking, short bushwalks and a mountain-bike skills course.
The site also includes a cafe and function/venue-hire facilities.
w: At the foot of the D'Aguilar ranges by Enoggera Reservoir, this low-key wildlife centre puts you nose-to-glass with platypus, lungfish, pythons and a cassowary in bush enclosures. Gateway to the national park beyond, it beats the big sanctuaries for quiet. Kayaks launch from the dam nearby. Real Queensland bush.

hook: Emerald rock pools and a tumbling cascade reached by rock-hopping up a creek in the Samford/Mount Glorious foothills.
facts: A waterfall and chain of swimming holes on Cedar Creek in the Samford / Mount Glorious area, on the western fringe of D'Aguilar National Park north-west of Brisbane.
Reached from the end of Cedar Creek Road, a little over 30 minutes from the Brisbane CBD, with car parking near a private-property boundary.
Access to the main falls is a roughly 2km return walk involving 15-30 minutes of rock-hopping up the creek.
The creek passes a series of small rock pools and cascades, prized for their emerald-green water and a rope/vine swing, before reaching the main falls.
There are no facilities at the main rock pools and falls; nearby Andy Williams Park provides toilets and electric BBQs.
Dogs are permitted on leash, and the rocks can be slippery so sturdy footwear is advised.
w: Up in the green folds below Mount Glorious, Cedar Creek tumbles over granite into a chain of clear rock pools, cold and deep enough to swim. Platypus work the quieter reaches at dawn; wallabies graze the banks. The forest drive up is half the pleasure. A subtropical swimming hole, an hour from the city.

hook: A deep river waterhole with an inland beach and rope swings on a gentle bend of the South Pine River.
facts: A riverside reserve at the end of Dugandan Road in Bunya, in Brisbane's north-west near Albany Creek, about 25 minutes north of the CBD.
The swimming hole sits at the junction of Bergin Creek and the South Pine River, on a gentle bend with a deep waterhole.
It is used for swimming, kayaking, canoeing and fishing, and features an inland beach and rope swings rigged by local families.
Facilities include a gas barbecue, picnic tables and amenities; gates open at sunrise and close at sunset each day.
Wildlife along the river includes possums, waterbirds, bush turkeys, occasional wallabies and platypus seen feeding along the banks in early morning.
Dogs are welcome but must be kept on a lead.
w: A deep, slow waterhole on the South Pine River at Bunya, screened by paperbarks and casuarinas — a local swimming and kayaking spot that feels miles from the suburbs around it. Families have cooled off here for generations, well away from any tourist map. Bring a kayak or just a towel. A secret bend of river, kept by the neighbourhood.

hook: A free Japanese garden of tea house, ornamental lake and boardwalks tucked inside Ipswich's heritage Queens Park.
facts: A Japanese garden within Queens Park, Ipswich, south-west of Brisbane, with free public entry.
It was designed in consultation with Ipswich's Japanese sister city, Nerima, and named for it.
The first stage opened on 17 May 2001, unveiled by Ipswich Mayor John Nugent and Nerima Mayor Saburo Iwanami to mark the sister-city relationship.
Features include a traditional model tea house with veranda used for Japanese tea ceremonies, an ornamental lake crossed by boardwalks, and winding paths.
The planting blends Japanese garden elements with Australian natives, including local Ipswich species, some rare and endangered, retaining a local identity.
It is maintained by Ipswich City Council; some pathways have been closed during garden rehabilitation works.
w: A pocket of stillness in Ipswich's Queens Park: an authentic Japanese garden of raked gravel, koi ponds, stone lanterns and a tea house, gifted by the sister city of Nerima in Tokyo. Winding paths and clipped maples reward a slow, quiet circuit. A short trip west of Brisbane, and worth it. Zen, transplanted to the Queensland bush.

hook: A hidden Fortitude Valley laneway of street art, boutiques and tiny bars wedged behind the old Shannon's building.
facts: A small laneway off Ann Street in Fortitude Valley, tucked behind the historic Shannon's building that houses the Zoo live-music venue.
It is lined with street art, boutiques, hair salons and small eateries, including Ben's Burgers and the Phoebe Paradise boutique.
It is one of a cluster of Valley laneways with California Lane and Bakery Lane (Ada Lane sits in the same hidden-laneway group).
Winn Lane, California Lane and Bakery Lane together host the monthly Valley Laneway Markets.
It functions as a creative hub mixing fashion, food, art galleries and live music in a single compact lane.
w: A narrow, scruffy-cool lane off Ann Street in Fortitude Valley, crammed with hole-in-the-wall bars, tiny shops and ever-shifting street art. Winn Lane and its neighbours — Bakery, California, Ada — form a little warren of the Valley's offbeat nightlife. Duck in and follow your ears. The kind of lane that rewards getting slightly lost.
